While SetPSDMetaChannels() is called beforehand, if it fails to properly validate or cap the channel count against the actual image dimensions or allocated resources, the loop will call ReadPSDChannel with out-of-bounds channel indices. ReadPSDChannel uses the channel index to determine which pixel channel to write to, so an excessive channel count could cause writes beyond allocated image channel buffers, leading to heap corruption.
findings.poc: Craft a PSD file where the layer record specifies an abnormally large number of channels (e.g., 255) but the actual image only supports a small number (e.g., 4 for RGBA). If SetPSDMetaChannels doesn't reject or cap this value, the loop will call ReadPSDChannel with channel indices 4 through 254, each of which reads a compression type and channel data, potentially writing pixel data beyond the bounds of the image's channel storage.

findings.title: Unchecked SetPSDMetaChannels return status allows partial setup
findings.description: While the return value of SetPSDMetaChannels is checked (status), if SetPSDMetaChannels partially succeeds (e.g., allocates some but not all meta channels before encountering an error), the subsequent loop may still attempt to read channel data for channels that were not properly initialized. The function may return MagickFalse but leave the image in an inconsistent state where some channels are allocated and others are not, leading to potential null pointer dereferences or use of uninitialized memory in ReadPSDChannel.
findings.poc: Craft a PSD file with a layer that requests a large number of channels that causes SetPSDMetaChannels to fail partway through allocation (e.g., due to memory pressure). The function returns MagickFalse, but the image may have partially allocated channel structures. If the error handling path doesn't properly clean up, subsequent operations on the layer image could access uninitialized or partially set up channel data.

findings.title: Missing dimension validation for PSD version 2 (PSB) files
findings.description: The code validates that PSD version 1 files have dimensions <= 30000, but performs NO validation for version 2 (PSB) files. Per the PSD specification, version 2 files support dimensions up to 300,000 x 300,000, but the code accepts any 32-bit value. A crafted PSB file with extremely large dimensions (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF x 0xFFFFFFFF) could bypass this check. While SetImageExtent() provides a secondary check against resource limits, the missing primary validation violates defense-in-depth and could lead to integer overflows in downstream calculations that use psd_info.rows/columns before or after SetImageExtent, or cause denial-of-service via excessive memory allocation attempts.
findings.poc: Craft a PSD file with signature '8BPS', version=2, reserved=0x000000000000, channels=1, rows=0xFFFFFFFF, columns=0xFFFFFFFF, depth=8, mode=3 (RGB). The dimension check is skipped because version != 1, allowing the extreme dimensions to pass through to SetImageExtent and subsequent processing.

findings.title: No validation of color mode value against expected range
findings.description: psd_info.mode is read as a 16-bit unsigned short from the file but is never validated to be within the range of valid PSD color modes (0-7 typically: Bitmap, Grayscale, Indexed, RGB, CMYK, Multichannel, Duotone, Lab). The switch statement handles specific modes but no default error case is shown. An unrecognized mode value would fall through the switch without setting min_channels or the colorspace appropriately, leaving psd_info.min_channels at its default of 3 and the image in an undefined colorspace state. This could lead to incorrect channel processing, buffer overreads when reading channel data (expecting 3+ channels but file provides fewer), or type confusion in downstream code.
findings.poc: Craft a PSD file with mode=0xFFFF (65535). The switch statement won't match any case, min_channels remains 3, and colorspace remains unspecified. Subsequent channel reading code may attempt to read 3+ channels from a file that doesn't provide them, causing out-of-bounds reads.

findings.title: Missing validation that channel count is appropriate for color mode
findings.description: While the total channel count is validated against MaxPSDChannels, there is no validation that the channel count is appropriate for the specified color mode. For example, RGB mode (mode=3) should have at least 3 channels, but the code accepts 1 channel with RGB mode. Similarly, CMYK mode should have at least 4 channels. The only mode-specific channel check is for IndexedMode (channels > 3 is rejected). This mismatch between channel count and color mode could cause buffer overreads when the code attempts to read more channels than exist in the file, or could cause incorrect pixel data interpretation.
findings.poc: Craft a PSD file with mode=3 (RGB), channels=1, rows=100, columns=100, depth=8. The min_channels will be set to 3 (default), but only 1 channel exists. When the code later attempts to read 3 channels of pixel data, it will read beyond the available data, potentially causing a crash or information disclosure.

findings.title: Ignored return value on reserved field read
findings.description: The return value of ReadBlob() when reading the 6-byte reserved field is explicitly cast to (void) and ignored. If the read fails or returns fewer than 6 bytes, the psd_info.reserved buffer may contain uninitialized or stale data. While the reserved field is typically not used in processing, if any code path later reads from psd_info.reserved, it could use uninitialized data. Additionally, the missing error check means a truncated file at this point would not be detected until the EOFBlob check later, potentially allowing processing to continue with partially-read header data.
findings.poc: Provide a PSD file that is truncated immediately after the version field (only 4+2=6 bytes total). The ReadBlob for reserved will fail, but the error is ignored. Subsequent reads may return garbage or default values, and the EOFBlob check at line ~2443 may not catch all cases if the file has just enough data to pass individual reads.

findings.title: Integer overflow in SeekBlob offset calculation
findings.description: The expression `offset+(MagickOffsetType) length` in the SeekBlob call can overflow. If `length` is an unsigned type (e.g., size_t) with a value exceeding SSIZE_MAX, the cast to `MagickOffsetType` (signed) produces a negative value. The resulting sum could be negative or unexpectedly small, causing SeekBlob to position the file pointer at an incorrect location. Subsequent parsing (ReadPSDMergedImage, ReadPSDLayersInternal) would then interpret file data incorrectly, potentially leading to heap buffer overflows, out-of-bounds reads, or other memory corruption in downstream parsing routines.
findings.poc: Craft a PSD file where the layer and mask information length field is set to a value near SIZE_MAX (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF on 32-bit or 0x7FFFFFFFFFFFFFFF+1 on 64-bit). When cast to MagickOffsetType (signed), this becomes negative, causing the seek target offset+length to wrap to an incorrect position. The subsequent ReadPSDMergedImage call will then parse data from the wrong file offset, potentially interpreting attacker-controlled data as image dimensions, channel counts, or buffer sizes.

findings.title: Unchecked SeekBlob return value leading to incorrect file position
findings.description: The return value of SeekBlob is explicitly discarded with `(void)`. If SeekBlob fails (e.g., seeking beyond file bounds or to an invalid offset due to the integer overflow above), the file position is undefined. Subsequent read operations (ReadPSDMergedImage at line 2670, ReadPSDLayersInternal at line 2676) will operate on unexpected data, potentially causing misinterpretation of file content as image metadata, leading to buffer overflows or crashes.
findings.poc: Provide a PSD file with a layer/mask information length that causes SeekBlob to seek past the end of file. Since the return value is not checked, execution continues with the file pointer at EOF or an unexpected position. ReadPSDMergedImage then reads garbage/zero data, potentially causing uninitialized memory use or incorrect buffer allocations.

findings.title: Potential use-after-free with icc_profile pointer during profile replication
findings.description: The `icc_profile` pointer is obtained from `image` via `GetImageProfile(image,"icc")` and then used in a loop calling `SetImageProfile` on subsequent images. If `image` and any `next` image share internal profile storage through copy-on-write semantics, calling `SetImageProfile` on `next` could trigger a COW detachment that reallocates `image`'s profile map storage, invalidating the `icc_profile` pointer. Subsequent iterations would then dereference freed memory.
findings.poc: Craft a PSD file that produces multiple output images where image->next shares profile storage with image (via COW). The icc_profile pointer obtained before the loop becomes dangling after SetImageProfile triggers COW detachment on the first iteration, causing use-after-free on subsequent iterations.

findings.title: Stale image_list_length after ReadPSDMergedImage may bypass safety checks
findings.description: The variable `image_list_length` is computed at line 2668 before `ReadPSDMergedImage` is called at line 2670. If `ReadPSDMergedImage` modifies the image list (e.g., adds or removes images), `image_list_length` becomes stale. The stale value is then used at line 2680 in the condition `(psd_info.has_merged_image == MagickFalse) && (image_list_length == 1) && (length != 0)`. An incorrect `image_list_length == 1` evaluation when the actual list length differs could cause `ReadPSDLayersInternal` to be called unexpectedly or skipped, potentially leading to incorrect image processing or bypassing of the InsufficientImageDataInFile safety check at line 2687.
findings.poc: Craft a PSD file where ReadPSDMergedImage partially succeeds but modifies the image list length. If image_list_length was 1 before the call but the actual list grows, the condition at line 2680 evaluates incorrectly, potentially skipping ReadPSDLayersInternal or entering unexpected code paths.

findings.title: Integer overflow in ssize_t cast within subimage loop
findings.description: The for-loop condition `i < (ssize_t) image_info->scene` casts a `size_t` value to `ssize_t`. If `image_info->scene` exceeds `SSIZE_MAX` (e.g., 0x80000000 on 32-bit), the cast produces a negative value, causing the loop to not execute despite the guard check `image_info->scene < (size_t) TIFFNumberOfDirectories(tiff)` passing. This logic bypass means the code skips subimage navigation entirely and processes the wrong TIFF frame, potentially leading to incorrect output or processing of unintended data.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF file and request a subimage index >= SSIZE_MAX (e.g., on 32-bit: image.tif[2147483648]). The guard check passes if TIFFNumberOfDirectories returns a value > 2^31, but the loop body never executes, causing the first directory to be processed instead of the requested subimage.

findings.title: Denial of Service via unbounded image allocation in subimage loop
findings.description: The subimage handling loop calls `AcquireNextImage()` for each iteration up to `image_info->scene` times, bounded only by `TIFFNumberOfDirectories()`. A crafted TIFF file with a very large number of IFD entries (e.g., chained directories) can cause `TIFFNumberOfDirectories()` to return a large value, leading to excessive memory allocation and potential OOM crash. Each `AcquireNextImage` allocates a full Image structure with associated resources.
findings.poc: Create a TIFF file with thousands of chained IFD directories. Request subimage processing (e.g., image.tif[50000]). The loop will allocate ~50000 Image structures, exhausting available memory.

findings.title: Use of uninitialized stack variables (height, width, rows_per_strip)
findings.description: The variables `height`, `width`, and `rows_per_strip` are declared as `uint32` but never initialized to default values. If subsequent `TIFFGetField()` calls fail to populate these variables (e.g., due to a malformed TIFF missing required tags), they will contain stack garbage. These values are typically used later in the function to calculate buffer sizes, allocate memory, and control loop bounds, potentially leading to buffer overflows, out-of-bounds reads/writes, or information disclosure of stack contents.
findings.poc: Craft a minimal TIFF file that is valid enough to pass initial parsing but missing the ImageWidth, ImageLength, or RowsPerStrip tags. The corresponding variables will retain stack garbage values, which may be used as dimensions for memory allocation and pixel processing loops in subsequent code.

findings.title: Potential infinite loop in TIFFNumberOfDirectories with circular IFD chains
findings.description: The call to `TIFFNumberOfDirectories(tiff)` on line 1285 walks the IFD chain to count directories. A malformed TIFF with circular IFD references (where a later directory's `next IFD` pointer points back to an earlier one) can cause this function to loop indefinitely or return an extremely large value. This leads to either a hang (infinite loop in libtiff) or the memory exhaustion issue described above.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF file where IFD #0's next pointer points to IFD #1, and IFD #1's next pointer points back to IFD #0. TIFFNumberOfDirectories will loop infinitely, hanging the process.

findings.title: Signed integer overflow in scene loop via size_t to ssize_t cast
findings.description: The loop condition `i < (ssize_t) image_info->scene` casts a `size_t` (unsigned) value to `ssize_t` (signed). If `image_info->scene` exceeds `SSIZE_MAX` (e.g., >= 2^31 on 32-bit or >= 2^63 on 64-bit), this cast invokes undefined behavior per the C standard. On typical implementations, the value wraps to a negative number, causing the loop to not execute when it should (logic bypass). Conversely, a compiler exploiting UB could optimize away the check entirely, potentially leading to unexpected control flow. The outer guard `image_info->scene < (size_t) TIFFNumberOfDirectories(tiff)` limits practical exploitation since TIFFNumberOfDirectories typically returns a small value, but on systems where tdir_t is uint32 and the TIFF is crafted to report many directories, the cast could produce a negative loop bound, skipping directory reads and leaving the image in an inconsistent state.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF file where TIFFNumberOfDirectories returns a value > SSIZE_MAX (requires uint32 tdir_t and circular/manipulated directory chain). Set image_info->scene to a value between SSIZE_MAX and TIFFNumberOfDirectories result. The cast (ssize_t)image_info->scene wraps negative, loop body is skipped, but code proceeds as if directories were read.

findings.title: Resource exhaustion via unbounded scene iteration
findings.description: The for-loop iterates up to `image_info->scene` times, calling `AcquireNextImage()` on each iteration. Each call allocates a new Image structure (which includes pixel caches, profiles, and other metadata). A user-controlled scene index (e.g., via filename `image.tif[65534]`) combined with a TIFF file reporting many directories via `TIFFNumberOfDirectories()` can cause allocation of tens of thousands of Image structures, exhausting available memory. There is no upper bound check on `image_info->scene` beyond the TIFFNumberOfDirectories comparison, and no incremental resource limit enforcement within the loop.
findings.poc: Create a TIFF with 65535 IFD entries (maximum for uint16 tdir_t). Request scene 65534 via filename 'image.tif[65534]'. The loop will call AcquireNextImage 65534 times, each allocating significant memory, leading to OOM denial of service.

findings.title: Incomplete resource cleanup in ThrowTIFFException macro
findings.description: The ThrowTIFFException macro only cleans up `pixel_info` and `quantum_info` before calling ThrowReaderException. Other resources that may have been allocated earlier in the function (such as `sample_info` obtained via TIFFGetField for TIFFTAG_EXTRASAMPLES, or `chromaticity` from TIFFTAG_PRIMARYCHROMATICITIES) are not freed. While `sample_info` is managed by libtiff internally, `chromaticity` is a user-allocated float pointer declared at line 1180 that could leak if the macro is invoked after chromaticity is set. This is a memory leak that could be exploited for resource exhaustion in a loop processing many malformed TIFF images.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF that sets chromaticity tags successfully but triggers an error shortly after (e.g., invalid bits_per_sample). The ThrowTIFFException macro will be invoked, leaking the chromaticity buffer allocated by TIFFGetField.

findings.title: Potential infinite loop in TIFFNumberOfDirectories with circular IFD chain
findings.description: The call to `TIFFNumberOfDirectories(tiff)` at line 1289 iterates through all IFD entries by following next-directory pointers. A crafted TIFF with a circular IFD chain (where a directory's next IFD offset points back to a previous directory) can cause this function to loop indefinitely or return an extremely large count. While modern libtiff versions have some protections, older versions or specific configurations may be vulnerable. If TIFFNumberOfDirectories returns an artificially large value, the subsequent comparison `image_info->scene < (size_t) TIFFNumberOfDirectories(tiff)` would pass for large scene values, enabling the resource exhaustion described in finding #2.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF where IFD #0's next IFD offset points to IFD #0 itself (offset 0 or the offset of IFD #0). TIFFNumberOfDirectories will loop indefinitely (older libtiff) or return a very large count, bypassing the scene bounds check and enabling massive memory allocation in the scene loop.

findings.title: Missing validation of width/height leading to integer overflow in buffer calculations
findings.description: The width and height values read from the TIFF file (via TIFFGetField for TIFFTAG_IMAGEWIDTH and TIFFTAG_IMAGELENGTH) are assigned directly to image->columns and image->rows without any bounds checking. A crafted TIFF file can specify extremely large dimensions (up to 2^32-1 for uint32). When downstream code computes buffer sizes as width * height * samples_per_pixel * (bits_per_sample/8), this multiplication can silently overflow on 32-bit systems (or even 64-bit with sufficiently large values), resulting in a much smaller allocation than expected. Subsequent image data reads would then write past the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. This is a classic integer-overflow-to-heap-overflow pattern in image parsers.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF file with IMAGEWIDTH=65536 and IMAGELENGTH=65536 and samples_per_pixel=4 and bits_per_sample=8. The required buffer is 65536*65536*4 = 16GB, but on a 32-bit system the multiplication overflows, resulting in a small allocation. The subsequent pixel data write overflows the heap. Alternatively, use values like width=0x40000000, height=0x40000000 which overflow to a small value when multiplied.

findings.title: Missing zero-dimension check for width and height
findings.description: The code does not validate that width and height are non-zero before assigning them to image->columns and image->rows. A TIFF file with IMAGEWIDTH=0 or IMAGELENGTH=0 will result in zero-dimension image, which can cause division-by-zero, zero-size heap allocations (which may return a non-NULL dangling pointer), or other undefined behavior in downstream image processing code that assumes positive dimensions.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF file with TIFFTAG_IMAGEWIDTH set to 0 and TIFFTAG_IMAGELENGTH set to 1 (or vice versa). The image->columns will be set to 0, and subsequent code that divides by columns or allocates rows*columns bytes will encounter undefined behavior.

findings.title: Depth set to 64 for IEEEFP without downstream compatibility check
findings.description: When sample_format is SAMPLEFORMAT_IEEEFP and bits_per_sample is 64, the validation at lines 1332-1337 allows this combination, and image->depth is set to 64 at line 1427. However, much of the downstream ImageMagick processing pipeline assumes depth is at most 32 (or in some code paths, at most 16 or 8). Setting depth=64 can lead to buffer overflows in code that allocates based on depth<=32 assumptions, or incorrect pixel processing due to type confusion between integer and floating-point representations.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF file with SAMPLEFORMAT=IEEEFP (value 3), BITSPERSAMPLE=64, and valid width/height. The image will have depth=64, which may overflow buffers in downstream quantum processing code that uses depth to compute pixel buffer sizes with the assumption depth<=32.

findings.title: Heap Buffer Over-Read in TIFF Colormap Processing
findings.description: When processing a TIFF image with PHOTOMETRIC_PALETTE, the code allocates an image colormap with `image->colors` entries (derived from `bits_per_sample` via `GetQuantumRange(bits_per_sample)+1`), then iterates over the TIFF-provided `red_colormap`, `green_colormap`, and `blue_colormap` arrays up to `image->colors` entries. However, there is no validation that the TIFF COLORMAP tag actually contains `image->colors` entries. A crafted TIFF file can declare a large `BitsPerSample` (e.g., 16, yielding 65536 expected entries) but provide a COLORMAP tag with far fewer entries. This causes out-of-bounds reads on the heap-allocated colormap arrays returned by libtiff's TIFFGetField, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF file with: BitsPerSample=16, PhotometricInterpretation=PALETTE, but a COLORMAP tag containing only 256 uint16 values per channel instead of the expected 65536. The code will iterate i from 0 to 65535, reading far beyond the actual colormap buffer. Using a tool like `tiffset` or a hex editor: 1) Create a valid palette TIFF with 8-bit colormap. 2) Modify the BitsPerSample tag from 8 to 16. 3) The COLORMAP tag still only has 256 entries per channel, but the code will attempt to read 65536 entries per channel.

findings.title: Floating-Point Precision Issue in Colormap Size Check
findings.description: The check `pow(2.0,1.0*bits_per_sample) <= MaxColormapSize` uses floating-point arithmetic to determine if the colormap size is within bounds. The `pow()` function can produce imprecise results for certain inputs due to floating-point rounding. If `pow(2.0, bits_per_sample)` evaluates to a value slightly less than or equal to `MaxColormapSize` due to rounding, but the true integer value `1 << bits_per_sample` exceeds `MaxColormapSize`, the code would proceed to allocate an oversized colormap. This could lead to excessive memory consumption or, combined with the OOB read above, amplify the impact. The integer computation `(1U << bits_per_sample)` should be used instead.
findings.poc: On systems where MaxColormapSize is not a power of 2, craft a TIFF with bits_per_sample such that pow(2.0, bits_per_sample) rounds down to <= MaxColormapSize but the true value exceeds it. For standard MaxColormapSize=65536 this is not practically exploitable since 2^16=65536 is exactly representable, but custom builds with different MaxColormapSize values could be vulnerable.

findings.title: Unchecked Return Value of SetImageColorspace
findings.description: The return value of `SetImageColorspace(image,image->colorspace,exception)` is assigned to `status` but the subsequent check `status&=(MagickStatusType) ResetImagePixels(image,exception)` combines it with ResetImagePixels using bitwise AND. If SetImageColorspace fails but ResetImagePixels succeeds, the combined status could still appear successful (or vice versa), potentially leaving the image in an inconsistent colorspace state. This could lead to incorrect image processing or subtle memory corruption in downstream operations that assume the colorspace was properly set.
findings.poc: Provide a TIFF file with an invalid or unsupported colorspace value that causes SetImageColorspace to fail, while ResetImagePixels succeeds. The combined status check may pass, allowing processing to continue with an improperly initialized colorspace.

findings.title: Integer Overflow in number_pixels calculation (image->columns * rows_per_strip)
findings.description: The calculation of `number_pixels` uses `MagickMax` with two arguments. The first argument explicitly casts `image->columns` to `MagickSizeType` (64-bit), but the second argument `image->columns * rows_per_strip` performs the multiplication without any cast. On 32-bit systems where `size_t` is 32-bit, this multiplication can overflow, producing a small value. Even on 64-bit systems, if both operands are sufficiently large (e.g., each near 2^32), the 64-bit product can overflow. The resulting undersized `number_pixels` is typically used to allocate buffers for reading image data, leading to a heap buffer overflow when the actual pixel data is read into the too-small buffer. The inconsistency between the first argument (cast to MagickSizeType) and the second (no cast) strongly suggests this is an oversight by the developer.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF file with: image->columns = 0x10000 (65536), TIFFTAG_ROWSPERSTRIP = 0x10000 (65536), samples_per_pixel = 1, bits_per_sample = 1. On a 32-bit system, image->columns * rows_per_strip = 0x100000000 which overflows to 0 in 32-bit arithmetic. The first MagickMax argument would be 65536*1*1 = 65536, so number_pixels = 65536. However, the actual strip contains 65536*65536 = 4GB of pixel data, causing a massive heap buffer overflow when reading. On 64-bit systems, use columns near 2^33 and rows_per_strip near 2^33 to overflow 64-bit multiplication.

findings.title: Undefined behavior with bits_per_sample=0 in number_pixels calculation
findings.description: When bits_per_sample is 0, the expression `log(bits_per_sample)/log(2.0)` evaluates to `log(0)/log(2)` = -infinity. Then `ceil(-inf)` = -inf, and `pow(2.0, -inf)` = 0.0. This makes the first argument to MagickMax equal to 0, so number_pixels is determined solely by `image->columns * rows_per_strip`. A bits_per_sample of 0 is invalid for TIFF image data, and subsequent code that uses bits_per_sample for buffer size calculations or bit-shift operations may divide by zero, access out-of-bounds memory, or allocate insufficient buffers. The lack of validation for bits_per_sample > 0 before this calculation is a security concern.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF file with bits_per_sample = 0, reasonable image dimensions (e.g., 100x100), and samples_per_pixel = 3. The pow() expression evaluates to 0, making the first MagickMax argument 0. number_pixels becomes image->columns * rows_per_strip. Later code that divides by or shifts by bits_per_sample will encounter undefined behavior (division by zero or shift by negative count), potentially crashing or corrupting memory.

findings.title: Floating-point precision loss in number_pixels calculation
findings.description: The first argument to MagickMax uses floating-point arithmetic: `(MagickSizeType) image->columns * samples_per_pixel * pow(2.0, ceil(log(bits_per_sample)/log(2.0)))`. The result of the pow() call is a double, which causes the entire multiplication chain to be performed in floating-point. For very large values of image->columns and samples_per_pixel, the double precision (53-bit mantissa) may not be sufficient to represent the exact integer result, leading to truncation or rounding. This could result in number_pixels being slightly smaller than the true value, potentially causing off-by-one or small buffer overflows. Additionally, the implicit conversion from double to MagickSizeType (unsigned integer) for very large doubles that exceed the integer range is undefined behavior in C.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF with image->columns = 0x200000 (2097152), samples_per_pixel = 255, bits_per_sample = 16. The pow expression yields 16.0. The product is 2097152 * 255 * 16.0 = 8,589,869,056.0. This is within double precision range, but for larger values (e.g., columns = 0x2000000 with samples_per_pixel = 65535), the product exceeds 2^53 and loses integer precision, potentially truncating to a smaller value.

findings.title: Inadequate Heap Overflow Sanity Check in ReadTileMethod
findings.description: The HeapOverflowSanityCheck validates rows * sizeof(*tile_pixels) which equals rows * 1 (since tile_pixels is unsigned char*), but the actual allocation uses extent = MagickMax(tile_size, rows * MagickMax(stride, length)). The sanity check does NOT protect against overflow in the real allocation expression. A crafted TIFF with large tile dimensions (e.g., rows=0xFFFF, stride=0x10001) can cause rows*stride to overflow on 32-bit or wrap on 64-bit, resulting in a small extent allocation. Subsequent tile data reads then overflow the heap buffer.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF with TILEWIDTH and TILELENGTH set to large values (e.g., 65535) such that rows * stride overflows size_t. The HeapOverflowSanityCheck(rows, 1) passes trivially, but the actual extent calculation overflows, leading to a small allocation and heap buffer overflow when TIFFReadEncodedTile writes tile data.

findings.title: Heap Buffer Overread via rows_per_strip=0 in ReadStripMethod
findings.description: When rows_per_strip is 0 (settable via TIFFTAG_ROWSPERSTRIP in a crafted TIFF), the variable rows_remaining (size_t) is set to 0 after reading a strip, then decremented to SIZE_MAX via underflow. This causes the condition (rows_remaining == 0) to never trigger again, so no new strips are read and p is never reset to strip_pixels. The pointer p advances by stride each iteration for image->rows iterations, eventually reading far past the allocated buffer in ImportQuantumPixels.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF with ROWSPERSTRIP tag set to 0. The extent allocation becomes MagickMax(strip_size, 0) = strip_size. After the first row, rows_remaining underflows from 0 to SIZE_MAX. The pointer p advances by stride each of image->rows iterations, causing ImportQuantumPixels to read heap memory beyond the strip_pixels buffer.

findings.title: Integer Overflow in number_pixels Calculation
findings.description: The expression number_pixels=(MagickSizeType) columns*rows performs the multiplication in uint32 arithmetic (since columns and rows are uint32) BEFORE the cast to MagickSizeType. If columns=65536 and rows=65536, the product overflows uint32 to 0, which is then cast to MagickSizeType. This can lead to incorrect size calculations if number_pixels is used downstream for buffer sizing or bounds checks.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF with TILEWIDTH=65536 and TILELENGTH=65536. The multiplication 65536*65536=4294967296 overflows uint32 to 0, making number_pixels=0. Should be (MagickSizeType)columns * (MagickSizeType)rows to avoid overflow.

findings.title: Potential Integer Overflow in Strip Extent Calculation
findings.description: The extent calculation extent=MagickMax(strip_size, rows_per_strip*MagickMax(stride,length)) can overflow on 32-bit platforms where size_t is 32 bits. If rows_per_strip is a large uint32 value and stride/length are also large, the multiplication wraps around producing a small extent value. This leads to an undersized allocation, and subsequent TIFFReadEncodedStrip or ImportQuantumPixels operations overflow the buffer.
findings.poc: On a 32-bit build, craft a TIFF with ROWSPERSTRIP=0x10000 and configure image dimensions/bits-per-sample so that stride is large (e.g., 0x10001). The product 0x10000*0x10001=0x100010000 overflows 32-bit size_t to 0x10000, causing a small allocation while the actual data written exceeds it.

findings.title: Insufficient HeapOverflowSanityCheck in Generic TIFF Method
findings.description: The HeapOverflowSanityCheck validates `image->rows * sizeof(*pixels)` but the actual allocation is `image->columns * image->rows * sizeof(uint32)`. The check omits the `image->columns` multiplier, meaning it fails to detect overflow in the full allocation size calculation. If `image->columns * image->rows * sizeof(uint32)` overflows while `image->rows * sizeof(*pixels)` does not, the sanity check passes but the subsequent allocation via AcquireVirtualMemory could be insufficient, leading to a heap buffer overflow when TIFFReadRGBAImage writes pixel data.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF file where image->rows is small enough that `rows * sizeof(*pixels)` doesn't overflow, but image->columns is large enough that `columns * rows * sizeof(uint32)` overflows the intermediate type. On systems where MagickSizeType multiplication doesn't prevent the issue, TIFFReadRGBAImage will write beyond the allocated buffer.

findings.title: Integer Overflow in Pointer Arithmetic for Generic Method Pixel Iteration
findings.description: The expression `p+=(ptrdiff_t) (image->columns*image->rows)-1;` computes `image->columns*image->rows` using the native `size_t` type without a 64-bit cast. On 32-bit systems or when the intermediate result exceeds `ptrdiff_t` range, this multiplication can overflow, causing `p` to point to an incorrect memory location. Subsequent pixel processing in the loop would then read from or write to out-of-bounds memory, leading to heap corruption or information disclosure.
findings.poc: On a 32-bit build, craft a TIFF with columns=65537 and rows=65537. The 32-bit multiplication overflows: 65537*65537=4295098369 which wraps to 131073 in 32-bit. The pointer p is advanced by only 131072*sizeof(uint32) bytes instead of the correct ~16GB, causing subsequent pixel reads to access memory before the intended end of the buffer.

findings.title: Missing Validation of TIFFReadTile Return Size Against Expected Data
findings.description: After calling TIFFReadTile, the code only checks for a -1 error return but does not validate that the returned `size` is sufficient for the subsequent processing loop. The loop iterates `rows_remaining` times, advancing the read pointer `p` by `stride` bytes each iteration, potentially reading up to `rows_remaining * stride` bytes from `tile_pixels`. If TIFFReadTile returns fewer bytes than expected (e.g., a short read due to a truncated/crafted TIFF), the loop reads beyond the actually-written data in tile_pixels. While the buffer is memset to zero initially, stale data from previous tile reads could be leaked, and if rows_remaining*stride exceeds the buffer extent, a heap over-read occurs.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF file with tile dimensions that cause TIFFReadTile to return a small positive size (e.g., a truncated tile), while rows_remaining and stride are derived from image/tile metadata that expect more data. The processing loop will read beyond the returned data, potentially past the tile_pixels buffer boundary.

findings.title: Potential Mismatch Between GetAuthenticPixels Extent and ImportQuantumPixels Processing Width
findings.description: GetAuthenticPixels is called with `columns_remaining` as the width, which can be less than the full tile width at image edges. However, ImportQuantumPixels is called with `quantum_info` that may be configured for the full tile width. If ImportQuantumPixels processes more pixels than the columns_remaining region allocated by GetAuthenticPixels, it would write beyond the authenticated pixel cache region, causing a heap buffer overflow. This is especially dangerous at the right edge of the image where columns_remaining < tile width.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF image whose width is not a multiple of the tile width. When processing the rightmost tiles, columns_remaining will be less than the tile width. If ImportQuantumPixels uses the full tile width from quantum_info instead of columns_remaining, it writes past the pixel cache region returned by GetAuthenticPixels.

findings.title: Buffer Over-Read/Over-Write in Reverse Pixel Iteration Loop
findings.description: The pixel reading loop iterates `image->columns` times while decrementing both the source pointer `p` and destination pointer `q` on each iteration. The loop reads from `*p` (via TIFFGetR/TIFFGetG/TIFFGetB/TIFFGetA) and writes to `q` (via SetPixel* functions) without validating that the iteration count matches the actual allocated buffer sizes. If a crafted TIFF file declares a larger `image->columns` value than the actual pixel data in the strip/tile, the loop will read before the start of the source buffer (buffer under-read) and write before the start of the destination pixel buffer (buffer under-write). The reverse iteration pattern (`p--`, `q-=GetPixelChannels(image)`) makes this particularly dangerous as it moves toward lower memory addresses, potentially accessing unmapped memory or corrupting adjacent heap metadata.
findings.poc: Craft a TIFF file with a large ImageWidth (columns) value in the IFD header but provide strip/tile data that contains fewer pixels than declared. For example, set ImageWidth=65535 but provide strip data for only 100 pixels. When the reverse pixel loop executes, it will iterate 65535 times, reading far beyond the allocated source buffer and writing far beyond the allocated destination buffer, causing heap corruption and potential code execution.

findings.title: Integer Underflow in SetImageProgress Scene Calculation
findings.description: The expression `(MagickOffsetType) image->scene-1` is passed to `SetImageProgress`. If `image->scene` is 0 (which can occur for the first frame in a multi-frame TIFF), the subtraction `image->scene-1` may underflow before the cast to `MagickOffsetType` depending on the unsigned type of `scene`. If `scene` is an unsigned type, this results in a very large positive value being passed, which could cause unexpected behavior in progress tracking or be used as an index/offset elsewhere leading to out-of-bounds access.
findings.poc: Craft a multi-frame TIFF where the first frame has scene number 0. When `AcquireNextImage` processes the second frame and calls `SetImageProgress(image, LoadImagesTag, (MagickOffsetType) image->scene-1, image->scene)`, the underflow occurs. If `image->scene` is of type `size_t` and equals 0, then `0-1` wraps to `SIZE_MAX` before the signed cast.

findings.title: Missing Validation of TIFFReadDirectory Return Value for Frame Counting
findings.description: The `TIFFReadDirectory` return value is checked for non-zero to determine if more frames exist, but the code doesn't validate whether the next directory/frame is well-formed before calling `AcquireNextImage`. A malformed subsequent frame could have inconsistent dimensions or properties that lead to buffer mismatches in subsequent iterations of the outer loop. The `more_frames` flag drives continued processing without sufficient validation of the new frame's integrity.
findings.poc: Create a multi-frame TIFF where the first frame is valid but the second frame's IFD contains corrupted dimension fields (e.g., ImageWidth=0 or extremely large). The code will attempt to allocate and process the second frame based on these corrupted values, potentially leading to zero-size allocations or integer overflow in buffer size calculations.

findings.title: TOCTOU Race Condition: File Reopened by Name After Validation
findings.description: The code reads and validates the first 128 bytes of the file (magic number check via heif_check_filetype), then closes the blob, and subsequently reopens the file by name using heif_context_read_from_file(heif_context, image->filename, ...). Between the validation and the second read, an attacker could replace the file (e.g., via a symlink swap) with a malicious file that would not pass the initial filetype check. This bypasses the safety validation and feeds arbitrary data to the libheif parser, potentially triggering parser vulnerabilities. The fix would be to read the entire file into memory first and use heif_context_read_from_memory instead.
findings.poc:
1. Create a valid HEIC file at /tmp/poc.heic
2. Run an ImageMagick convert in one thread: convert /tmp/poc.heic out.png
3. In another thread, after the magic check passes but before heif_context_read_from_file executes, replace /tmp/poc.heic with a symlink to a crafted malicious HEIF file or a non-HEIF file
4. The libheif parser processes the replaced file without the filetype validation gate

findings.title: Memory Leak of 'ids' Array on Error Paths Within Loop
findings.description: The ids array is allocated via AcquireQuantumMemory at line 725. Within the for-loop, if AcquireNextImage fails (GetNextImageInList returns NULL) or if heif_context_get_image_handle fails (IsHEIFSuccess returns MagickFalse), the code sets status=MagickFalse and breaks out of the loop. However, the ids pointer is not freed on these error paths before the break. While the code is truncated and cleanup may exist after the visible portion, the break statements within the loop bypass any such cleanup. This can be triggered repeatedly to exhaust memory (memory leak DoS).
findings.poc: Craft a HEIF file with multiple top-level images where one image handle is corrupted or invalid. When heif_context_get_image_handle fails for a non-primary image, the break is hit without freeing ids. Repeat processing to leak memory.

findings.title: Missing Image Size Limit on Older libheif Versions (Decompression Bomb)
findings.description: The maximum image size limit (heif_context_set_maximum_image_size_limit) is only applied when LIBHEIF_NUMERIC_VERSION >= 1.19.0 (compile-time check). On systems with older libheif versions, no size limit is enforced, allowing a crafted HEIF file to declare extremely large dimensions. This can lead to excessive memory allocation and potential denial of service when the image is decoded. The max_size variable is computed but never used on older library versions.
findings.poc: On a system with libheif < 1.19.0, craft a HEIF file with declared dimensions of 100000x100000 pixels. The decoder will attempt to allocate ~40GB of memory for the decoded image buffer, causing OOM or system instability.

findings.title: Unchecked Negative Return from heif_context_get_number_of_top_level_images
findings.description: heif_context_get_number_of_top_level_images returns int, which is stored in ssize_t count. While the 'count > 1' check would reject negative values, if the function returns INT_MAX, the subsequent AcquireQuantumMemory((size_t)count, sizeof(*ids)) attempts to allocate a very large buffer (INT_MAX * sizeof(heif_item_id), potentially ~8GB). Although AcquireQuantumMemory should return NULL for excessive allocations (and the NULL check exists), the attempt itself could cause resource exhaustion. Additionally, the cast (int)count when passing to heif_context_get_list_of_top_level_image_IDs is redundant but safe given the source type.
findings.poc: Craft a HEIF container that reports an extremely large number of top-level image IDs (close to INT_MAX). This forces a massive allocation attempt before the NULL check catches it.

findings.title: Ignored return value of ReadHEICDepthImage
findings.description: The return value of ReadHEICDepthImage() is not checked or assigned to the 'status' variable. If ReadHEICDepthImage fails (e.g., due to a malformed depth image in the HEIC file), the 'status' variable retains its previous value (likely MagickTrue), causing the error to go undetected. This means a partially initialized or corrupt image object could be returned to the caller, potentially leading to memory corruption, information disclosure, or crashes when downstream code operates on the invalid image. The subsequent check on 'status' at line 763 is ineffective for catching errors from ReadHEICDepthImage.
findings.poc: Craft a HEIC file with a valid primary image but a corrupted/invalid depth image (e.g., malformed auxiliary image metadata). When ReadHEICDepthImage fails internally, the error is silently ignored, and the image is returned in an inconsistent state. The fix would be: status=ReadHEICDepthImage(image_info,image,image_handle,exception);

findings.title: Potential integer overflow in scene bounds check
findings.description: The expression 'image_info->scene + image_info->number_scenes - 1' on line 748 could theoretically overflow if both values are large unsigned integers. If image_info->scene + image_info->number_scenes wraps around, the bounds check could evaluate incorrectly, potentially allowing iteration beyond the intended range or causing an unexpected break. In practice, ImageMagick typically validates these values earlier, but the arithmetic itself is not overflow-safe.
findings.poc: Set image_info->scene to a large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF) and image_info->number_scenes to 2, causing the sum to wrap to 0, making the comparison evaluate incorrectly.

findings.title: Heap buffer overflow via missing length validation after blob_size adjustment
findings.description: When GetBlobSize() returns 0 (as it does for non-seekable streams like stdin/pipes), and the RIFF chunk size in the WEBP header is crafted so that `delta = length - blob_size` equals exactly 12 or 20, the adjusted `length` becomes 0. The code only validates `length >= 12` BEFORE the adjustment (line ~558) but never re-checks after `length -= delta`. This leads to: (1) AcquireQuantumMemory(0,1) may return a non-NULL pointer to a zero-byte allocation (e.g., glibc malloc(0) returns a valid pointer), (2) memcpy(stream, header, 12) writes 12 bytes into the zero-byte buffer causing a heap buffer overflow, and (3) `length-12` in ReadBlob underflows to SIZE_MAX-11, causing a massive out-of-bounds write via ReadBlob(image, SIZE_MAX-11, stream+12). The WriteWebPLSBWord(header+4, length-8) also suffers an integer underflow writing 0xFFFFFFF8 into the header.
findings.poc: Craft a minimal WEBP header (12 bytes): 'RIFF' + little-endian uint32 chunk_size=4 + 'WEBP'. Pipe this to ImageMagick via stdin: `printf 'RIFF\x04\x00\x00\x00WEBP' | convert - output.png`. GetBlobSize() returns 0 for the pipe. length = 4+8 = 12. delta = 12-0 = 12 (passes check). length becomes 0. If AcquireQuantumMemory(0,1) returns non-NULL, memcpy writes 12 bytes to a 0-byte heap allocation -> heap buffer overflow. For delta=20 variant, use chunk_size=12 (length=20).

findings.title: Integer underflow in length-8 passed to WriteWebPLSBWord after adjustment
findings.description: After `length -= delta`, if length becomes 0 (when blob_size=0 for non-seekable streams), the expression `length-8` in WriteWebPLSBWord(header+4, length-8) underflows. Since length is size_t (unsigned), 0-8 wraps to SIZE_MAX-7. WriteWebPLSBWord writes this as a 32-bit value (0xFFFFFFF8) into the stack-local header buffer. While this doesn't directly corrupt memory (the write targets a valid stack buffer), it produces a semantically incorrect RIFF chunk size that propagates into the stream buffer, potentially confusing downstream parsers and indicating a missing validation step.
findings.poc: Same as the heap buffer overflow PoC: pipe a crafted 12-byte WEBP header with chunk_size=4 via stdin. After adjustment, length=0, and WriteWebPLSBWord(header+4, 0-8) writes the underflowed value.

findings.title: Denial of service via unbounded memory allocation from crafted RIFF chunk size
findings.description: The RIFF chunk size read from the WEBP header via ReadWebPLSBWord() is a 32-bit value. After adding 8, `length` can be up to ~4GB (0xFFFFFFF8). The code allocates `length` bytes via AcquireQuantumMemory without any upper bound check specific to reasonable WEBP file sizes. A crafted WEBP file with a large RIFF chunk size can cause ImageMagick to attempt allocating gigabytes of memory, leading to denial of service through memory exhaustion, OOM kills, or excessive swap usage. The only mitigation is that the allocation may fail and return NULL, which is handled, but the attempt itself consumes resources.
findings.poc: Create a WEBP file with RIFF header declaring chunk_size = 0x7FFFFFF0 (length = 0x7FFFFFF8, ~2GB). The file itself can be small. `convert crafted.webp output.png` will attempt a ~2GB allocation.

findings.title: Unvalidated Integer Configuration Parameters Passed to WebP Encoder
findings.description: Multiple SetIntegerOption calls set WebP configuration fields (partitions, method, segments, filter_strength, filter_sharpness, filter_type, pass, preprocessing, sns_strength, target_size, alpha_compression, alpha_filtering, alpha_quality, show_compressed, thread_level) directly from user-controlled image options without any bounds validation before passing to WebPValidateConfig. For example, configure.partitions should be 0-3, configure.method should be 0-6, configure.segments should be 1-4. If WebPValidateConfig does not catch all invalid values (especially in older libwebp versions), out-of-range values could cause out-of-bounds array accesses or buffer overflows within the libwebp encoder internals. Historical CVEs (e.g., CVE-2023-4863) have shown that invalid WebP configuration can lead to heap buffer overflows.
findings.poc: convert input.png -define webp:partitions=9999 -define webp:method=9999 -define webp:segments=9999 output.webp

findings.title: Unvalidated Float Conversion for target_PSNR Allows NaN/Infinity Values
findings.description: The target_PSNR configuration field is set from user input via StringToDouble cast to float without any validation. A user can supply special floating-point values such as 'inf', '-inf', 'nan', or extremely large numbers. These special float values can cause undefined behavior in the WebP encoder's rate-control algorithms, potentially leading to infinite loops, division by zero, or incorrect memory allocations. WebPValidateConfig may not check for NaN/infinity in floating-point fields.
findings.poc:
convert input.png -define webp:target-psnr=inf output.webp
convert input.png -define webp:target-psnr=nan output.webp
convert input.png -define webp:target-psnr=1e308 output.webp

findings.title: near_lossless Parameter Set from Unvalidated Quality Value
findings.description: When WEBP_ENCODER_ABI_VERSION >= 0x020e, configure.near_lossless is set directly from image->quality without range validation. The near_lossless parameter in libwebp is expected to be in the range 0-100. If image->quality is set to a value outside this range (e.g., a very large number or negative value), the near_lossless field receives an out-of-range value. While quality >= 100 triggers lossless mode, near_lossless is still set to the raw quality value, which could be 10000 or -1, potentially causing unexpected encoder behavior.
findings.poc:
convert input.png -quality 99999 output.webp
convert input.png -quality -50 output.webp

findings.title: Missing Total Pixel Count Overflow Check on Image Dimensions
findings.description: The code checks individual dimension limits (columns > 16383, rows > 16383) but does not check the product columns * rows for overflow or excessive memory allocation. With both dimensions at 16383, the total pixel count is ~268 million, which at 4 bytes/pixel (RGBA) requires ~1GB. While this doesn't overflow a 64-bit size_t, it could cause excessive memory consumption (DoS) or overflow a 32-bit size_t on 32-bit builds (16383*16383*4 = 1,073,610,756 which exceeds UINT32_MAX on multiplication with bytes-per-pixel).
findings.poc:
convert -size 16383x16383 xc:white output.webp  # Allocates ~1GB RAM
# On 32-bit: columns*rows*4 could overflow size_t during buffer allocation

findings.title: Missing NULL check for CoalesceImages result leads to potential buffer overflow
findings.description: When CoalesceImages() is called (line 1239) because animation frames have differing dimensions, its return value is never checked for NULL. If CoalesceImages fails (e.g., due to memory allocation failure), coalesce_image remains NULL, and the code falls through to the else branch (line 1250-1251), calling WriteAnimatedWEBPImage with the original image that has mismatched frame dimensions. WebP animation requires consistent frame sizes; processing frames with inconsistent dimensions under the assumption of uniformity can lead to buffer overflows in the encoder — reading past the bounds of smaller frames or writing past allocated buffers.
findings.poc: Craft a multi-frame image (e.g., GIF or MIFF) where frames have different dimensions (e.g., first frame 100x100, second frame 50x50). Force CoalesceImages to fail by exhausting memory (e.g., via resource limits). The encoder will attempt to process mismatched frames, potentially reading out-of-bounds pixel data from smaller frames.

findings.title: Fragile ownership transfer between WebPMemoryWriter and WebPData creates latent double-free
findings.description: In the non-animated success path, webp_data.bytes is set to writer.mem (line 1259-1260), transferring buffer ownership implicitly. WebPDataClear(&webp_data) at line 1270 frees this buffer via WebPFree(). However, writer.mem still holds a dangling pointer to the freed memory. While WebPMemoryWriterClear is not called on writer in this success path, the ownership model is broken: (1) if WriteWEBPImageProfile reallocates webp_data.bytes, writer.mem points to freed memory; (2) any future code change adding cleanup of writer would trigger a double-free. Additionally, WebPDataClear and WebPMemoryWriterClear may use different deallocators in custom-allocator configurations, causing heap corruption.
findings.poc: Encode a single (non-animated) WEBP image. In the success path, after WebPDataClear frees webp_data.bytes (== writer.mem), writer.mem is a dangling pointer. If a signal handler, error path, or future code change invokes WebPMemoryWriterClear(&writer), a double-free occurs. With custom allocators, the mismatch between WebPDataClear's deallocator and WebPMemoryWriter's allocator can corrupt heap metadata.

findings.title: Unconditional WebPDataClear on potentially uninitialized or stale webp_data
findings.description: WebPDataClear(&webp_data) is called unconditionally at line 1270, even in error paths where webp_data may not have been properly populated. In the non-animated failure path (WriteSingleWEBPImage returns MagickFalse), writer is cleared via WebPMemoryWriterClear but webp_data is never assigned — it retains whatever prior value it held. If webp_data was not zero-initialized earlier in the function, WebPDataClear will attempt to free a garbage pointer, causing heap corruption or a crash. Even if zero-initialized, if a prior iteration left stale data in webp_data, it could be incorrectly freed.
findings.poc: Trigger WriteSingleWEBPImage failure (e.g., by providing an image with unsupported color space or exceeding resource limits). If webp_data contains stale bytes pointer from a prior state, WebPDataClear will free invalid memory. This can be triggered reliably by crafting an image that passes initial validation but fails during WebP encoding.

findings.title: DestroyImageList after WriteAnimatedWEBPImage may invalidate referenced pixel data
findings.description: After WriteAnimatedWEBPImage encodes coalesce_image into webp_data, DestroyImageList(coalesce_image) is called immediately (line 1247). If WriteAnimatedWEBPImage internally stores lazy references to the image's pixel data (e.g., for later profile embedding or if encoding is deferred), destroying the source image creates dangling pointers. While typical WebP encoding is synchronous and produces a self-contained byte stream, the immediate destruction without verifying webp_data is complete is a fragile pattern that could become exploitable if the encoding implementation changes.
findings.poc: Provide a multi-frame image with varying dimensions to trigger the coalesce path. If WriteAnimatedWEBPImage defers any pixel access (e.g., for multi-pass encoding), the subsequent DestroyImageList frees the pixel buffers, making webp_data contain or reference freed memory. Subsequent WriteWEBPImageProfile or WriteBlob operations would then access freed data.

findings.title: Resource Leak / File Descriptor Leak on Error Path
findings.description: When ReadOnePNGImage() returns NULL (indicating an error), the function returns NULL without calling CloseBlob() on the image. Unlike the ThrowReaderException macro used elsewhere (which properly closes the blob and destroys the image), this error path simply returns NULL. If the original image (from AcquireImage) was not destroyed by ReadOnePNGImage, both the image memory and the open file descriptor are leaked. An attacker can trigger this by crafting a PNG file that passes the initial signature/size checks but causes ReadOnePNGImage to fail, potentially exhausting file descriptors through repeated invocation (DoS).
findings.poc: Create a PNG file with a valid 8-byte signature and size >= 61 bytes, but with corrupted internal chunk data that causes ReadOnePNGImage to fail and return NULL. Repeatedly process this file to leak file descriptors: convert malicious.png out.png && convert malicious.png out.png ... until 'Too many open files' error occurs.

findings.title: Potential Use-After-Free via mng_info->image Dangling Pointer
findings.description: The original image pointer is stored in mng_info->image at line 3953. Then ReadOnePNGImage() is called, which may internally free/destroy the original image and return a new image pointer (or NULL). After ReadOnePNGImage returns, MngReadInfoFreeStruct(mng_info) is called. If MngReadInfoFreeStruct accesses mng_info->image (e.g., for cleanup or unlinking), it would dereference a dangling pointer to the already-freed original image, resulting in a use-after-free. This pattern of storing a pointer in a structure, potentially invalidating it via a called function, and then using the structure is a classic UAF pattern in ImageMagick.
findings.poc: Craft a PNG that causes ReadOnePNGImage to replace the original image with a new one (e.g., via MNG/APNG frame handling), freeing the original. If MngReadInfoFreeStruct then accesses mng_info->image, heap metadata corruption or controlled code execution may be possible. A PNG with specific chunk sequences that trigger image replacement in ReadOnePNGImage would be needed.

findings.title: Weak File Size Validation Bypass for Non-Seekable Streams
findings.description: The check `GetBlobSize(image) < 61` is intended to reject files too small to contain a valid PNG datastream. However, GetBlobSize() may return 0 or an incorrect value for non-seekable input sources (pipes, stdin, network streams). For such streams, this check would incorrectly reject valid PNG data (if GetBlobSize returns 0) or could potentially allow processing of truncated data (if GetBlobSize returns a stale/cached value). This weakens the validation gate and could allow malformed/truncated PNG data to reach ReadOnePNGImage, increasing attack surface for vulnerabilities in the PNG parsing logic.
findings.poc: Pipe a truncated PNG (e.g., 30 bytes with valid signature) through stdin: cat truncated.png | convert - out.png. Depending on GetBlobSize behavior for pipes, the size check may be bypassed, allowing the truncated data to reach ReadOnePNGImage.
